data.title: How Does The Federal Reserve Set Interest Rates?
data.description:
By Lydia Mashburn Newman, Paul Mueller

Introduction

People in financial markets and in the media regularly track Federal Reserve announcements and forecast what the Fed’s interest rate target will be in the future. But when people speak of the Federal Reserve increasing or decreasing the interest rate, to what are they referring? What interest rates does the Fed target? How does the Fed target them? What are the effects of its interest rate targets? This Explainer addresses those questions.

What the Fed Targets

The Federal Reserve targets an interest rate called the federal funds rate (FFR). The FFR represents the interest rate banks charge one another for overnight borrowing of reserves in the federal funds market. Reserves are money. They include cash that banks keep on hand as well as the money banks hold in accounts at the Fed.

Banks lend and borrow reserves in the federal funds market based on their short-term money needs—these include money to meet depositor withdrawals, to pay out loans made to consumers, and to cover regulatory requirements. How many reserves banks lend or borrow depends on how much money they need to meet these liquidity obligations. Banks with extra reserves lend; those with shortfalls borrow. The amount of lending and borrowing of reserves in the federal funds market affects the FFR.

The Fed can influence this market and the FFR by adding newly created reserves or extracting reserves from the banking system. When the Fed creates more reserves, the FFR falls. This is called expansionary monetary policy because it stimulates economic activity. When the Fed reduces the supply of reserves, the FFR tends to rise. This is called contractionary monetary policy because it reduces available credit and dampens economic activity.

For most of its history, until 2008, the Federal Reserve influenced the FFR market by adding reserves to, or subtracting reserves from, the banking system through open market operations. These operations involved the Fed buying bonds with newly created bank reserves, thereby lowering the FFR, or selling bonds and extinguishing bank reserves, thereby increasing the FFR. Since reserves were relatively scarce in the pre-crisis system, even small interventions by the Fed had predictable effects on the FFR.

In modern times, rather than using open market operations, the Fed relies on three interest rates that it directly sets, or administers, to increase or decrease reserves in the banking system. Through these rates, the Fed influences banks’ borrowing and lending activity in the FFR market. The result is an FFR within the Fed’s interest rate target.

How the Fed Targets the FFR

The three rates the Fed sets to reach its FFR target range are: the discount rate, interest on reserve balances (IORB), and overnight reverse repurchase agreements (ON RRP). The most important are IORB and ON RRP.

IORB is the primary tool the Fed uses to achieve its FFR target. It is the rate the Fed pays banks for reserves kept on deposit at the Fed. For contractionary monetary policy, the Fed sets this rate above what banks can typically earn elsewhere, encouraging them to keep excess reserves at the Fed rather than lending them, thereby exerting upward pressure on the FFR. For expansionary monetary policy, the Fed sets this rate below what banks can earn elsewhere, encouraging them to put more reserves to use in the economy, thereby reducing the FFR.

The ON RRP is the lower bound of the Fed’s FFR target range. It is used to reinforce the Fed’s IORB rate by offering a similar, but lower, interest rate to non-bank financial institutions such as hedge funds, money market mutual funds, insurance companies, and government-sponsored enterprises. These institutions cannot earn IORB because they cannot keep reserves at the Fed. However, they can influence overnight lending markets with their own reserves. Therefore, the Fed offers ON RRP to non-banks to ensure these institutions keep in line with the Fed’s FFR target.

The discount rate is set above IORB and marks the high end of the Fed’s FFR target range. It is the rate that the Fed charges banks for borrowing directly from the Fed at the discount window. Since no bank would pay more than this rate to get reserves, it creates a ceiling on the Fed’s FFR target. Historically, borrowing directly from the Fed carried significant stigma – people in the market interpreted it to mean the bank was high-risk, distressed, or both. Since becoming part of the Fed’s interest rate setting system, however, the discount window stigma has declined considerably.

Through August 2025, the Fed’s target FFR range was 4.25–4.5 percent. To achieve its target, the Fed set IORB at 4.4 percent, ON RRP at 4.25 percent, and the discount rate at 4.5 percent. As a result, the effective FFR has landed roughly in the middle of IORB and the ON RRP at 4.33 percent throughout 2025 (Figure 1).

Figure 1: Fed Interest Rate Targets Since 2023

Source: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, FRED Graph

How Interest Rate Targets Interact with the Fed’s Balance Sheet and Banks

While adjusting IORB and ON RRP allows the Fed to manage the FFR in what appears to be a precise fashion, it requires the banking system to have ample reserves. An ample reserve environment means banks have more than enough reserves to meet their short-term money needs with plenty left over to be sensitive to the Fed raising or lowering the IORB. To set rates in this fashion, the Fed has had to dramatically increase both the amount of reserves in the banking system and the size of its balance sheet.

For example, total reserves held by banks historically averaged $50 billion or less. As the Fed started paying interest on reserves in 2008, that number jumped to nearly $1 trillion. It reached a peak of almost $4.2 trillion during the COVID-19 pandemic. By mid-2025, it had declined but remained elevated at more than $3.3 trillion.

Maintaining an ample reserve environment also requires the Fed to maintain a balance sheet significantly larger than under the previous interest rate setting regime. To generate sufficient reserves, the Fed purchases bonds from financial institutions, which increases the size of the Fed’s balance sheet. Historically, the Fed’s balance sheet stood at less than $900 billion. It ballooned to more than $2 trillion during the global financial crisis, only to be dwarfed by a fourfold increase to nearly $9 trillion during the pandemic. In mid-2025, the Fed’s balance sheet stood at roughly $6.6 trillion.

Such large amounts of money hanging on the sidelines of the banking system can quickly lead to inflationary pressures once banks choose to lend those reserves instead of keeping them at the Fed. Combined with the Fed’s large balance sheet, the current system raises concerns about whether the central bank’s interest rate policies lead to either financial repression or credit allocation—areas typically deemed inappropriate for monetary authorities to engage.

How the Federal Funds Rate Affects the Economy

Although the Fed primarily targets and influences the overnight rate of interest, its actions affect other interest rates because they are either indexed to, or built upon, the overnight rate. Additionally, investors shift their holdings of debt and their interest rate expectations based on what the Fed does and signals it will do with the FFR.

Three important consumer interest rates broadly affected by Federal Reserve interest rate policy are the thirty-year mortgage rate, credit card interest rates, and the interest rate on vehicle loans (Figure 2). Commercial and corporate borrowing are also affected by changes in the FFR target.

Figure 2: Fed Funds, 30-Year Mortgage, 4-Year Auto, & Credit Card Rates Since 2000

Source: Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, FRED Graph

Interest rate changes have many effects on the economy. They change the relative cost for purchases that involve substantial amounts of debt, such as cars and houses. For example, even if there is no change in the sticker price of a $20,000 car, the car can become much more expensive if the buyer uses financing.

If the buyer finances the $20,000 at 5 percent amortized over five years, he will have a monthly payment of $377. But if he finances the $20,000 at 8 percent, his monthly payment will be $406 – a 7.4 percent increase. Although $29 a month may not seem like much, it adds more than $1,700 to the total cost over five years. If you add a zero—a $200,000 mortgage versus a $20,000 car loan—the increased financing cost becomes $290 per month or $3,480 per year. With a 30-year mortgage, that amounts to an increase of more than $100,000.

Interest rates affect the cost of purchasing homes and cars, credit cards, and business loans. Beyond that, they serve as important signals and prices for businesses and investors, acting as a key input for determining the costs and potential returns of entrepreneurial ventures. A slight change can make all the difference between a worthwhile endeavor and a failed one. With many interest rates influenced by the FFR, small changes to the Fed’s FFR target can impact large segments of the economy.

Conclusion

People watch the Fed closely because its decision to change its FFR target affects financial markets and the economy in many ways. Overall, the Fed’s current interest rate setting system has given the appearance of precise control, using IORB and ON RRP to manage the amount of money in the economy. But it has not been without costs. Banks and financial institutions are more dependent on the Fed and its decisions when it comes to how much lending and borrowing takes place in the economy. The current system also requires a large Fed balance sheet and higher levels of reserves. The result is a much larger Fed footprint in the economy that can dampen the efficient allocation of credit as determined by markets and poses risks to price stability—as evidenced by inflation hitting a 40-year high post-pandemic in 2022.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CarMax Turns Into An Car Crash After Quarterly Results
data.description:
Thesis: KMX’s poor quarterly results took a big bite out of the company’s valuation. Following the steep decline, I suspect share prices are bottoming out and may recover a bit. It is, in my opinion, too late to sell, although if the economy deteriorates or we see evidence of further auto loan delinquencies rising, share prices could contract further.

Unfortunately, poor quarterly results have turned CarMax, Inc. (NYSE:KMX) into my worst stock recommendation of the year. CarMax’s shares have taken a deep dive following the release of Q2 2026 results. In premarket trading, stock prices were already down double digits, and shortly out of the gate, share prices had plummeted roughly 25%. Comparable-store sales for used vehicles were down 6.3% in Q2, while used retail units overall were down 5.4%. All told, used retail unit revenue was down 7.2% YOY.

Still, EPS also took a big hit, declining nearly 25% from a year prior. With share prices declining by a similar amount, the PE valuation is correcting in line with decreased performance. Encouragingly, YTD (fiscal year 2026) is still up 11% YOY, topping $2. Ultimately, the most important question, in my opinion, is whether the poor quarterly performance was largely due to a temporary pothole in the road owing to the pull-forward effect potentially caused by the trade war, or if it’s part of a sustained deterioration.

It wasn’t all bad news, with gross profits per unit in line with last year’s Q2 results. The gross profit per retail used unit weighed in at $2,216, while the wholesale unit gross profit was $993, and the Extended Protection Plan margin per retail unit came in at $576. Still, the retail used vehicle gross profit declined 7.6% YOY. Further, Selling, General, and Administrative (SG&A) expenses declined 1.6% to $601.1 million, and KMX is looking to trim a further $150 million over the next 18 months.

Loans Remain My Chief Concern

KMX keeps a lot of its loans on its books, and as I noted in my last write-up on the company, deteriorating loan quality could emerge as a serious threat. We have seen subprime auto loan delinquencies tick up in recent months, and it’s possible conditions will deteriorate. Auto finance loan income decreased 11.2% in Q2, falling to $102.6 million. The provision for loan losses jumped from $112.6 million a year ago to $142.2 million this year. The 2022 and 2023 loan vintages are currently the worst performers but do remain “highly profitable,” according to CarMax. The company tightened underwriting standards in April 2024, and so far, loans generated afterward remain on track with expectations.

In total, the allowance for loan losses of $507.3 million hit 3.02% of auto loans held for investment as of August 31, 2025, an increase from 2.76% in May 31, 2025. The large portfolio of auto loans and risk of rising delinquencies were my chief concerns when I last reported on KMX back in early August. The Q2 results certainly don’t allay the fears, although it is at least a bit encouraging that underwriting tightening is leading to improved performance.

What About Other Car Companies?

I haven’t yet really begun to look through the data, but what about other car companies? General Motors (NYSE:GM) and Ford (NYSE:F) both stumbled in early morning trading. I can’t say for sure that it was due to the issues with CarMax, but I doubt its struggles helped. Then there’s Carvana (NYSE:CVNA) and AutoNation (NYSE:AN), which have been gaining ground in recent weeks. They also suffered a rough go out of the gate. I wouldn’t necessarily tell investors to sell based on CarMax’s struggles, but it’d be wise to do further research.

As shown above, CNVA and AN both suffered some morning trading losses, although the declines were relatively moderate, at least compared to the sharp retreat suffered by KMX. Unlike CarMax, Carvana sells off many of its loans to investors. This should help reduce risks for Carvana if auto loan delinquencies continue to rise. I am not sure about AutoNation, but I would advise AN investors to take a close look at the company’s position in terms of auto loans.

Why I Could Be Wrong

Right now, I am thinking that CarMax’s struggles are part of a larger trend that will impact the economy as a whole. The cool-off is, in my opinion, likely due to the pull-forward effect of tariffs evaporating and leading to cooling demand. That said, KMX’s struggles could be due to deeper issues, and it’s possible that the company's struggles will continue apace or even accelerate in the weeks and months ahead.

The second quarter GDP results have been revised upward, showing significant growth. By and large, I believe the economy did do well. I also suspect we’ll see the economy slow a bit in the third quarter, and believe that there’s a very high chance the economy will continue to slow going forward. However, overall, I have been feeling confident that the economy will continue to expand. If the pull-forward effect was stronger than I previously realized, and that led to inflated expectations, it’s possible the market could be due for a correction even if the economy continues to grow.

CarMax’s performance alone doesn’t prove these worries correct, but I will be paying closer attention. Other issues, such as a historically high Shiller CAPE ratio, are also concerning. I am not hitting the panic button, but the market does feel more unstable than it did a month ago.

Key Takeaway: Sales Breakdown Is A Major Concern

The quarterly results weren’t good. There’s not really a major silver lining here as far as I can see. Efforts to cut costs will help in the long run, but that’s a potential future tailwind, not something we can currently mark in the books. CarMax is probably close to bottoming out unless another major headwind emerges. I wouldn’t rush to sell right now, but if you worry that CarMax’s stumble could portend issues with other companies, I’d suggest digging deeper.

data.title: Freeport-McMoRan: Too Much Uncertainty After Grasberg Incident
data.description:
As investors we often tend to neglect certain idiosyncratic risks that are both rare and hard to predict. The recent event at Freeport-McMoRan's (NYSE:FCX) Grasberg Block Cave mine (GBC) in Indonesia is a perfect example of just that and unfortunately it is an incident that came with the loss of human life too.

According to FCX management, on September 8, around 800,000 metric tons of mud entered the mine and went through various mine levels. So far two fatalities have been identified with five more people still missing.

A force majeure has been declared after what was supposedly a mine landslide as rescue operations continue and the Indonesian government is now in the process of investigating the cause of the accident.

What that means is that there are still lots of uncertainties related to the future of the mine, and that is why FCX's stock price reacted by falling 17% yesterday after a press release regarding the accident was released.

This is a stark reminder that mining operations, especially those conducted underground, come with significant risks for anyone involved. The incident would also raise questions regarding safety and the need for companies to reinvest more of their profits back into the business as a way to reduce risk of accidents.

While it is too early to speculate about the overall impact of the event, there are a number of factors that should be taken into account.

Firstly, the Grasberg minerals district includes open-pit and three underground mines - the Grasberg Block Cave (GBC), the Deep Mill Level Zone (DMLZ) and Big Gossan. The incident occurred at GBC, which is by far the largest in terms of reserves.

According to the recent press release, the incident at GBC happened at one of the five production blocks, but damaged infrastructure needed to support other production areas at GBC.

The whole Grasberg district is quite large in terms of expected production, both for copper and gold.

The systemic importance of the Grasberg asset for FCX was highlighted during the last conference call and it should be kept in mind that as of Q2 2025 there were ongoing discussions with the Indonesian government about an extension of FCX operating rights.

Grasberg operations and district is our cornerstone asset. Second largest copper mine in the world. In this most recent quarter, we had a net credit for operating cost of $1 a pound. This asset has enabled us to create the modern Freeport. With the smelter nearing completion, we are progressing our discussions with the Indonesian government about extending our operating rights beyond 2041.

Source: FCX Q2 2025 Earnings Transcript

The impact on FCX Q3 2025 results seems to be relatively low with anticipated 4% drop of copper sales and 6% for gold.

For the third quarter of 2025, FCX’s consolidated sales are expected to be approximately 4% lower for copper and approximately 6% lower for gold than July 2025 estimates.

Source: FCX Website

Having said that, however, we should keep in mind that the accident occurred in September, which is the last month of Q3. More importantly, however, there's still significant uncertainty regarding the reopening of the different blocks at GBC and other underground mines. Some scenarios were provided by FCX's management (see the extract below), but the final outcome is still uncertain.

Currently, PTFI expects the unaffected Big Gossan and Deep MLZ mines could restart operations by mid-fourth quarter 2025, with a phased restart and ramp-up of the GBC mine beginning in the first half of 2026. As a result, PTFI fourth quarter 2025 sales of copper and gold would be insignificant (previously estimated sales of 445 million pounds of copper and 345,000 ounces of gold).

In the first half of 2026, a phased restart and ramp-up of GBC could, initially commence in three production blocks - “PB2” and “PB3”, followed by a third production block “PB1S” in the second half of 2026 and the balance of “PB1C” in 2027. This schedule would target the return to pre-incident estimates in 2027.

Under this phased restart and ramp-up scenario, which is subject to a number of factors and could change, PTFI production in 2026 could potentially be approximately 35% lower than pre-incident estimates (previous production estimates for 2026 approximated 1.7 billion pounds of copper and 1.6 million ounces of gold).

Source: FCX Website

For these reasons, it might be too early to accurately estimate the overall impact of the disaster, but investors should also keep in mind that Indonesia Operations represent a significant portion of FCX's revenue and operating income.

What To Expect?

Even if we ignore the potential impact on FCX's share price from the recent events, the stock was already showing signs that it is not following the price of copper. For example, copper futures are up significantly over the past year, while FCX has been lagging behind.

This gap is now likely to widen further due to FCX's profitability headwinds and the fact that the market has reacted to a potential supply disruption stemming from the closure of the second largest copper mine in the world.

I covered all that in further detail about a year ago, when I showed why investors have been unimpressed with FCX's quarterly results and why all eyes have been focused on expected movements in the price of copper. Some of the positives that I noted were Indonesia's expansion and some progress being made on reducing costs. The former is now hardly a reason to get excited about FCX's future returns.

Moreover, it seems that the market does not fully account for the now expected drop in profitability of FCX. Although there is no solid relationship between margins and the price/sales multiple on a historical basis, the current multiple of above two does not seem to suggest a more meaningful drop in EBITDA margins in the coming quarters.

All that creates the necessary conditions for FCX to remain under pressure for the time being. The Non-GAAP earnings multiples (on a historical and forward basis) are also elevated even though forward estimates have not been updated yet to reflect the recent earnings release.

Investor Takeaway

The Indonesian mine incident is a stark reminder of the risks involved in mining operations, especially in extremely large underground projects. Based on the available information, it seems that H2 2025 results will be under significant pressure with uncertainties likely to linger into 2026 as well. For the time being it seems prudent to stay away from the stock as more details come in and the overall impact is being quantified. The level that FCX now trades at also does not suggest that the market is fully pricing-in the significant risks that lie ahead.

data.title: Forge Global: Favorable View Of Bank Alliance And Profitability Prospects
data.description:
My rating for Forge Global Holdings, Inc. (NYSE:FRGE) remains a "Buy." I think it will turn EBITDA-positive in '26. Its latest cooperation with a financial services organization is a wise move.

FRGE's "Intellectual Property" or "IP" commercialization and business acquisition were detailed in my earlier July 14, 2025, write-up.

Positive On Recent Collaboration With Tech-Focused Financial Institution

FRGE established an alliance with "Silicon Valley Bank" ("SVB") in 3Q25. According to the announcement, this involves the recommendation of "Forge’s private liquidity solutions" to the latter's non-listed depositors/borrowers to "manage their cap table, diversify their investor" mix.

The Type Of Enterprises That SVB Works With

As per the chart presented above, FRGE's newly added partner has a growing client base comprising of the leading "fintech" and "AI" businesses.

I think the firm would now have an additional channel to promote its services. In its 10-K, Forge highlighted that "our growth requires continued adoption of our (private market) platform by new customers and increased usage by current customers."

Also, the group should have gained market share because of this latest development.

It disclosed at Oppenheimer's (OPY) "28th Annual Technology, Internet & Communications Conference" (S&P Capital IQ transcript) last month that SVB "had a previous relationship with one of our (Forge's) competitors." The bank eventually switched to FRGE with an "exclusive partnership."

Imagine a privately held company intends to help its staff cash out their shares. Forge will be the only one referred, if the former turned to "Silicon Valley Bank" for advice.

Separately, some of FRGE's existing platform companies also borrow from SVB. This opens up cross-selling opportunities.

The Key Features For Its "Next Generation Marketplace" Or "NGM"

Forge plans to embed the above-mentioned NGM solution into its collaborators' systems. "Next Generation Marketplace" is described by the company as an upgraded "platform" to "execute private market trades," which was created with "partner integration" in mind.

For example, it will be very convenient for SVB's enterprise users to access information on private share transactions with their digital banking accounts.

This translates into incremental data-related topline contributions for FRGE in the near term. In my mid-July piece, I noted that its high-single-digit percent of "revenues (generated) from the data business" was still low compared to Intercontinental Exchange's (ICE) 25%.

Looking ahead, it can use NGM-integration as a selling point to get more banks on board to play a similar connector role as "Silicon Valley Bank." Financial firms would be happy to increase user stickiness with an expanded all-in-one online platform.

Profitability Turnaround Next Year Is A Realistic Goal

My July 14 article mentioned that FRGE's listed comparables "SoFi Technologies (SOFI), ICE, and Coinbase (COIN)" boasted relatively more demanding price-to-sales, or P/S, multiples. This is still the case.

Forge has been EBITDA-negative in every single quarter following its March '22 IPO. It has been penalized with a modest 2x P/S ratio.

Intercontinental, COIN, and SOFI are all profitable at the EBITDA level. They are deservingly trading in the 9x-11x price-to-revenue range.

I'm encouraged by the management's comments at the August Oppenheimer event that they have "a commitment to driving that (2026 EBITDA) breakeven."

Its EBITDA loss shrank from 1Q25's -$8.9 million to 2Q2025's -$5.4 million. The consensus estimate was wider at -$8.7 million. It has been moving in the right direction.

FRGE hasn't specified whether it will be EBITDA-positive in one of the quarters for FY26 or the full-year period. But my take is that this should happen sooner rather than later for a number of reasons.

One factor is the scaling up of its operations in Europe.

The group derived 7% of its FY2024 turnover from non-U.S. regions. It's reasonable to assume that the international segment was unprofitable last year because of negative operating leverage.

At OPY's investment forum in the prior month, FRGE indicated, "our ("cash") burn in that investment in Europe will have been extinguished coming out of 2025," as per prevailing "volume" trends.

My prediction is that the firm's European division should witness better profitability in the subsequent year with an enlarged sales base to absorb non-variable expenses.

Another thing to consider is the consolidation of its new acquiree's financials.

I drew attention to FRGE's "takeover of Accuidity Capital Management, or ACM, "a specialized asset management firm focused on private market investing," in my July-2025 update.

Forge has guided that the ACM transaction, which was done in early-July, is "accretive to EPS." This isn't a surprise, as fund managers typically earn high-margin recurring fee income.

Also, it's worth looking at the impact of FRGE's cost management initiatives.

The company shared at the conference organized by OPY that it has realized "efficiency gains" by shifting "some of our offshoring technology development in-house."

Given the specialized nature of Forge's business, which adds complexity, the firm could have paid a premium to engage third-party IT vendors. It's good to know that FRGE can do some of these on its own and produce savings at the same time.

You Would Watch These Risks

FRGE's relationship with SVB might turn sour, and the expected synergies may not be realized. This can deter Forge from cooperating with others in the same way.

The stock's valuation discount to peers could persist, assuming that its EBITDA-breakeven timeline is pushed backwards.

My Closing Remarks

FRGE is still a "Buy" in my opinion.

I'm positive on its operating profit prospects and the latest partnership with a bank.

Its capital appreciation potential is +60% (3.2/2.0), assuming mean reversion. Its current and 3-year average P/S metrics are 2.0x and 3.2x, respectively, as per S&P Capital IQ data.

data.title: Wall Street Lunch: Strong Economic Reports Dent Rate Cut Hopes
data.description:
Listen below or on the go on Apple Podcasts and Spotify

Three strong economic reports give the Fed doves pause. (0:15) CarMax gets clobbered. (1:55) High-flying Oracle gets a rare bear. (2:18)

This is an abridged transcript of the podcast:

Our top story so far, this morning’s economic data just threw up a big speed bump on the easy money expressway. Initial jobless claims, durable goods orders and the final GDP revision all came in hawkish.

Wells Fargo economists said: “A data deluge this morning gives the U.S. economy a new lease on life.”

“Second quarter GDP, in hindsight, is stronger than it first appeared in light of robust consumer service spending and more robust business fixed investment,” they said. “August durable goods orders suggest the cap-ex momentum carried into Q3. Jobless claims fell for both first-time filers and those re-filing for existing benefits.”

Q2 GDP was perhaps the most eye-catching, even though that’s well in the rearview mirror. At the final print, the economy rose at +3.8% pace, up from the prior estimate of +3.3%.

Prices were also revised higher. The core PCE price index, which excludes food and energy, was revised up to 2.6% from 2.5% in the second estimate.

August durable goods orders rose 2.9%, compared with the -0.3% decrease expected and -2.7% in the prior month. Excluding transportation, new orders gained 0.4% last month, confounding expectations for a 0.1% decline.

And weekly jobless claims dropped unexpectedly to 218,000, well below the 238,0000 consensus and 232,000 the week before. Continuing claims dipped to 1.926 million from 1.928 million, lower than the 1.93 million consensus.

Following the data, the expected amount of Fed cuts this year fell to 38 bps from about 43 bps before the numbers hit. Treasury yields continued their recent march higher, with the 10-year yield (US10Y) touching 4.2% for the first time since the start of the month. And the dollar index (DXY) hit a three-week-high.

Among active stocks, CarMax (KMX) is plunging after falling short of consensus estimates with its second-quarter earnings report.

The company saw revenue decline 6% to $6.59 billion during what it said was a challenging quarter. That tally missed the consensus mark by $430 million. Total retail used vehicle unit sales decreased 5.4% to 199,729 compared to a year ago.

Oracle (ORCL) caught a Sell rating from Rothschild & Co Redburn.

Despite Oracle's role in the massive Stargate Project with OpenAI, the Rothschild says the market is overestimating the value of Oracle's contracted cloud revenue. It set a price target on the stock of $175, which is about 40% less than its current value.

And Rosenblatt analyst Chris Brendler started coverage on digital retail trading platform Webull (BULL) with a Buy rating, pointing to its international expansion and as well as a friendlier U.S. crypto regulatory environment.

"Crypto trading peaked at 20% of Webull's revenuesin the last cycle, and while it likely won't approach this level anytime soon, crypto is a key driver of our above-consensus estimates," Brendler said.

In other news of note, Aldi is making another aggressive move in the U.S. grocery sector by rebranding its private label portfolio and launching its first-ever namesake brand. The rebrand will take place over the next few years. The Germany-founded company said the move is a direct response to customer feedback.

More than 90% of Aldi products are private label. Several brands will be replaced with the Aldi name, while brands like Clancy’s, Simply Nature and Specially Selected will remain on shelves with modernized branding. Other items like “Red Bag Chicken” will adopt shopper-given nicknames.

Aldi's core strategy focuses on offering low prices through private labels, efficiency, and cost controls. The narrow focus has helped to boost Aldi's global brand recognition in the era of inflation-weary consumers.

And in the Wall Street Research Corner, BofA strategist Savita Subramanian says the S&P 500 (SP500) is now trading as if it were the new risk-free rate.

The index trades rich on 19 out of 20 metrics, with four key measurements – market cap to GDP, price to book, price to operating cash flow and enterprise value to sales – hitting all-time highs.

“Buying stocks at these multiples feels bad, but there are good ways (sales/EPS/GDP booms) and bad ways (price declines) to resolve this seemingly untenable situation,” she said.

The index now features companies with “a lower debt to equity ratio than prior decades,” reducing one traditional source of market risk.

“Perhaps we should anchor to today’s multiples as the new normal rather than expecting mean reversion to a bygone era,” Subramanian said.

data.title: Sendas Distribuidora: Not Done From Deleveraging Gains
data.description:
Currently Brazil's second-largest supermarket chain, Sendas Distribuidora (OTC:ASAIY), popularly known as Assaí Atacadista, has been a major beneficiary of a very favorable scenario for cash-and-carry (C&C), reaping the rewards of rapid store expansion across the country over the last decade.

Initially, Assaí's expansion plan, financed by a high debt load, ended up putting pressure on the company's results due to the large number of stores in the process of maturing and the disbursements necessary to support the process (in a highly volatile interest rate environment in Brazil). In fact, the very tight correlation between Assaí's results and interest rate fluctuations in Brazil (Selic) was one of the main points of attention in the thesis I highlighted in my last coverage almost two years ago.

Over the past five years, Assaí's performance has been poor, reaching the peak of skepticism at the end of last year, with interest rates in Brazil reaching 15%, putting high pressure on the company's leverage. This year, however, signs of stabilization in the Selic rate have already corrected the ADR price significantly, jumping more than 100% year-to-date.

But I don't believe that ASAIY's rebound has stopped there. As stores mature, the company is reducing its CapEx, exercising excellent cost control, and generating increasingly better free cash flow, paving the way for a beautiful deleveraging process with a concrete target for 2025.

Estimating that it is quite feasible for Assaí to continue reaping successes on the path to deleveraging in the next two quarters, and with growth estimates pointing to solid numbers this year and in the following years, I believe that Buy here is still warranted.

On ASAIY's Rate-Sensitivity

Quite simply (and somewhat obviously for C&C companies), Assaí behaves like an extremely rate-sensitive Brazilian consumer stock.

The ADR price tends to move inversely to tight domestic monetary policy. Therefore, the higher the Selic rate, the greater the pressure on ASAIY. Conversely, rate cuts or support tend to boost the ADR's price performance.

As can be seen in the chart below, Assaí's behavior (blue line) has typically been inversely related to Brazil's interest rate (pink line) in recent years, confirmed in the lower line with a negative correlation through fluctuations below zero in periods of tightening.

So, as a rule of thumb, this negative correlation that occurs in periods of Selic under pressure can serve as an early signal for entry positions.

Bringing it to the present context, at the moment, the interest rate (Selic) in Brazil stands at 15%, maintained since July, after a high of 10.5% since July last year.

As we can see in the chart below, post-pandemic inflation, which exceeded 10%, was controlled by massive rate hikes from 2% in March 2021 to 13.8% in September 2022, taking effect from that period and reaching the inflation target tolerance range (3% ±1.5%) a year later, in September 2023 (as shown by the blue arrow)—which served as support for rates to fall to 10.5% in mid-2024.

The problem is that, as shown by the red arrow above, contrary to expectations that inflation would remain within the tolerance range with a Selic rate of 10.5%, inflation rose throughout 2024, until in H2 it exceeded the tolerance range again for the first time since Sep/23. The result was a more hawkish Brazilian Central Bank, and rates were raised from 10.8% at the end of 2025 to 15% by then.

As projections indicate that, with stable rates, inflation may converge to the tolerance range starting in February 2026, with inflation expected to reach 4.39% per year, the market has been pricing in a possible scenario of rate cuts since the beginning of this year, or at least that the peak of monetary tightening has already passed.

Therefore, assets such as ASAYI, which are extremely rate-sensitive, have seen their performance jump sharply this year, largely above other Brazilian equities represented by EWZ's ETF (EWZ).

On ASAIY's Strategy and Leverage

The main reason for the extreme sensitivity of Assaí's thesis to interest rates is precisely because debt is one of the central points of the company's thesis—although not necessarily the only one.

Much of Assaí's aggressive growth over the past decade (gross revenue CAGR of 24%) has been financed by debt, with total debt increasing by 54% since 2020.

Assaí's "growth formula" combined expansion (new stores/volume) with financing via the debt/debenture market, which works as long as cash generation and margins support debt servicing.

So much so that today, Assaí has a 6-8% share of food retail in Brazil within a 20-25% C&C market, consolidating its dominance, especially in the C&C market in Brazil.

And while over the last five years, thanks to an increase in debt, revenues have expanded by 74.6%, EBITDA generation has grown by only 10% over the same period, leaving Assaí a highly leveraged company.

In 1Q23, for example, debt/EBITDA reached 4.7x, while currently, in 2Q25 (the most recent quarter), the company has 3.17x, aiming for a 2.6x in 4Q25.

This deleveraging target of 2.6x in the next two quarters is based on the following points:

  • Lower CapEx compared to previous years, with Assaí managing to dedicate 90% of EBITDA generation to debt reduction;
  • Discipline in working capital and inventory to free up cash;
  • Focus on controlling expenses, with SG&A virtually stabilized over the last decade at least;
  • Occasional prepayment of debt and potential refinancing have helped to optimize interest costs and accelerate leverage reduction.

Note how the evolution of Assaí's stores over the last decade, as a result of intense CapEx, has positively converged in EBITDA margin gains from 4.1% in 2011 to 7.6% in the last twelve months, which is extremely relevant in a super competitive and complex food retail segment in Brazil.

On Valuations

Based on the market's consensus revenue estimate of $15.04 billion (R$79.71 billion), and the EBITDA margin remaining at the same level of 7.6% (EBITDA of R$6.05 billion), and assuming a forward P/S of 0.17x, if Assaí manages to reach its deleveraging target for Fiscal 2025 to 2.6x (vs. 3.17x at the moment), this would imply a reduction in net debt from R$13.80 billion to ~R$11.3 billion.

If a constant EV is maintained, this would, in theory, raise the implicit market value of the ADR from $2.56 billion to $3.2 billion—an upside here of ~25%.

Of course, this figure depends heavily on the maintenance of the EV, as well as the use of real net debt and the EBITDA adopted. However, I believe that, based on a stable/falling interest rate scenario in Brazil, the cost of capital will tend to favor Assaí, which I estimate to be no lower than 12% today.

Risks and Final Remarks

Sendas Distribuidora, or rather Assaí Atacadista, triple-digit gain over the course of this year, does not mark the end of the upside line for the company.

Simply from an ongoing deleverage process that seems to be well consolidated to be fulfilled with feasible targets for this year would put a relevant upside to the thesis that I still don't believe the market has priced in accordingly.

A further macro recovery for Brazilian equities, supported by maintaining/falling interest rates, mainly supported by inflation coming in lower and within the Central Bank of Brazil's target, due to FX tailwinds (a weaker USD vs. BRL), contributes to an optimistic outlook for food retail companies.

It's important to note, however, that the Brazilian economy is volatile, and a reversal in the yield curve could drastically put Assaí's deleverage plan in a bad light, causing an unbridled sell-off of the ADR in a matter of days.

In particular, I believe that this macro scenario, although plausible, does not configure the current reality, and I am much more inclined towards an ongoing recovery on Brazilian equities, as in the case of Assaí, than the other way around.

That said, the confluence of a favorable macro plus Assaí's very organized and stable operating momentum converging into a deleveraging progress makes it a Buy rating desirable in my view, at least for the short-term (~6 months range).

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Kinder Morgan: A Natural Gas Gem With A 4% Yield
data.description:
Kinder Morgan (NYSE:KMI) is a rapidly-growing midstream platform with considerable distributable cash flow and earnings strength. The energy enterprise is growing quickly in its core natural gas business and it has added billions of dollars to its project backlog in recent years… especially in highly promising regions such as the Permian Basin. I like that Kinder Morgan is doubling down on its natural gas business, which by far makes the biggest contribution to the midstream platform’s earnings before interest and depreciation. The firm's shares also remain attractively valued and have revaluation potential in the context of growing energy demand in the U.S. The dividend is well-supported by estimated DCF and the energy enterprise should have no problems growing its dividend next year.

Previous rating

I previously rated shares of Kinder Morgan a buy -- A Top Natural Gas Bet For Growth And Yield (Rating Upgrade) -- due to the midstream platform 1) Growing its distributable cash flow, 2) Having very decent dividend coverage, and 3) Likely benefiting from incremental energy demand driven by Data Centers. While the energy enterprise in the past went overboard with debt-financed acquisitions -- which resulted in a dividend cut in 2016 -- I like that the midstream company now has very strong dividend coverage. The growth backlog indicates that Kinder Morgan has a filled pipeline with projects that have the potential to make incremental DCF contributions in the future.

Natural gas-heavy footprint in key operational areas

Kinder Morgan is one of the largest midstream businesses and pipeline operators in North America. With a market cap of $61.8B, Kinder Morgan competes against other behemoths in the midstream industry such as Enterprise Products Partners (EPD), Energy Transfer (ENB) and MPLX (MPLX).

Kinder Morgan owns an extensive pipeline network that it uses to transport energy raw materials, such as natural gas and refined products, to consumer end-markets. The pipeline operator owns ~66,000 miles of natural gas pipelines and has other complementary assets as well, including refined products pipelines, terminals and storage facilities. The midstream platform has made an effort especially to grow its footprint in the high-potential Permian Basin, which connects producers to both end markets as well as export facilities along the U.S. Gulf Coast.

Natural gas pipelines is the largest segment for Kinder Morgan and it contributed $1,347M to the midstream platform's adjusted earnings before depreciation and amortization in the second quarter, representing 65% of all segment EBDA and showing 10% year-over-year growth. The firm's total EBDA increased 6% year-over-year to $2,081M in Q2'25 and the natural gas business is by far the one with the fastest growth.

While there are midstream companies that are larger than Kinder Morgan, the midstream platform is nonetheless a heavyweight in the North American energy market. Kinder Morgan transports approximately 40% of all domestic natural gas production to consumer end markets, which makes the energy enterprise an indispensable pillar of the U.S. energy market.

Demand for U.S. natural gas is expected to grow rapidly in the years ahead as the industrial base grows and relatively new parts of the U.S. economy -- such as the Data Center sector -- are expected to be major catalysts for energy demand growth. In fact, business consulting firm McKinsey is projecting that U.S. Data Centers will more than double their share of total U.S. power between 2025 until 2030. Earlier work from McKinsey also stated that the U.S. Data Center demand was set to expand at a 10% pace annually through 2030, highlighting sustained growth tailwinds for the firm's natural gas business.

As of the end of the second-quarter, Kinder Morgan's project backlog totaled $9.3B, showing a $1.2B increase since the end of FY 2024 and a $7.9B increase compared to FY 2021 levels. It is mainly the firm’s natural gas projects (92%) that are responsible for the addition of incremental backlog. With natural gas demand expected to grow consistently going forward, I believe Kinder Morgan has considerable development potential in this business.

These natural gas projects tend to be mainly expansions of already existing and operational assets. Most of these pipeline expansions will go in-service between FY 2026 and FY 2030, creating a long-term growth driver for Kinder Morgan's natural gas EBDA as well as distributable cash flow.

Kinder Morgan’s valuation

The midstream platform is currently valued at an enterprise-value-to-EBITDA ratio of 11.4X, which makes it the second-most expensive midstream play after Canadian energy champion Enbridge (ENB), whose shares are trading at an EBITDA multiplier of 13.0X. Enbridge also has a strong capital framework and is growing through acquisitions, which has resulted in me rating the energy enterprise a strong buy.

Enterprise Products Partners -- my all-time favorite MLP given the firm’s impressive 27-year distribution growth history -- is trading at an enterprise-value-to-EBITDA of 10.0X while MPLX is priced at a 10.2X EV-to-EBITDA ratio. The industry group average here is 11.2X.

Kinder Morgan is therefore trading slightly above the industry group average EBITDA multiplier, but the company is seeing strong, double-digit earnings growth in its core business (natural gas), is adding new assets to its pipeline network, and has rock-solid dividend coverage: according to its capital framework, Kinder Morgan plans with distributable cash flow of $2.34/share in FY 2025, meaning the midstream enterprise calculates with a dividend coverage ratio of 2.0X. For comparison, Enterprise Products Partners, which is by far the most reliable and consistent payer in the midstream industry, had a distribution coverage ratio of 1.6X.

Risks with Kinder Morgan

The biggest risk for Kinder Morgan relates to a potential slowdown in U.S. natural gas demand as well as a deteriorating dividend coverage profile. Energy demand projections are supported by a growing utilization of U.S. data centers, so I consider it highly unlikely that we are going to see a deceleration of growth in the U.S. midstream energy sector. What would get me to change my mind about Kinder Morgan is if the enterprise scaled back growth investments/CapEx in the natural gas business, which is seeing the strongest earnings growth in the portfolio.

Final thoughts

Kinder Morgan is a rapidly growing natural gas champion in the U.S. midstream energy industry that is managing to add new growth projects to its capital budget. Natural gas-related growth projects are key to the expansion of the firm's project backlog, which in turn is going to benefit from the growth of the Data Center industry. Further, Kinder Morgan expects 2.0X dividend coverage in FY 2025, and with key measures like natural gas EBDA growing 10% Y/Y in the second quarter, I believe Kinder Morgan will continue to prioritize growth in this specific segment.

While Kinder Morgan's shares are slightly more expensive than the average midstream platform, I like that Kinder Morgan is adding new assets/pipelines to its portfolio and pushing its natural gas development business, all of which are going to make incremental DCF contributions going forward.

Manager Commentary

As of 06/30/2025

“At a granular level, we believe geopolitical tensions and tariffs favor smaller, more localized companies with local supply chains.”

Cedar Street Asset Management

Market in Review

Financial markets experienced varying degrees of volatility during the second quarter of 2025, as governments and economic policymakers adjusted to new priorities and trade policies of the new Trump administration, while de-emphasizing geopolitical tensions. Global equities, as measured by the MSCI World Index, dropped by more than 10% following Trump’s tariff-oriented “Liberation Day” announcements, but sharply rebounded to finish the quarter with a gain of 11.6%. Yields on the U.S. 10-year Treasury finished at 4.23%, not far from where they started the quarter at 4.21%, despite a 60-basis-point, intra-quarter move. Meanwhile, the U.S. dollar declined by about 7% for a total decline of 10.7% for the year.

In response to heightened volatility and lower inflationary pressures, most developed market central banks opted to lower monetary policy rates even as the U.S. Federal Reserve (“Fed”) maintained its rate at a level that is among the highest in the developed world. For most non-U.S. developed markets, the combination of lower rates and currency appreciation meant that growth-style equities largely outperformed value-style equities and small-cap companies generally outperformed large-cap companies. In our view, when interest rates decline, investors tend to prefer growth equities, and when currencies appreciate, exporters (which tend to be large-cap companies) tend to underperform their small-cap peers.

In non-U.S. developed markets, the combination of currency appreciation, declining interest rates, and outperformance of both small-cap and large-cap equities relative to U.S. equities, suggests a continuation of the preference for non-U.S. financial assets that began in the first quarter. While elements of heightened speculative fervor may still exist, especially within AI-themed investments, in the U.S., we believe the demand for non-U.S. financial assets is gaining momentum.

Expense ratio information is as of the Fund’s current prospectus, as supplemented. Gross expenses are the Fund’s total annual operating expenses. The net expense ratios for this fund are subject to a contractual management fee waiver and/or expense limitation agreement, excluding interest expense and acquired fund fees and expenses (if any), through 02/28/2026.

Portfolio Performance

During the second quarter, the Harbor International Small Cap Fund (Institutional Class, “Fund”) returned 14.64%, underperforming its benchmark, the MSCI EAFE Small Cap Index, which returned 16.59%.

We believe the primary factors driving underperformance were interest rate cuts by most developed market central banks and fund flows into passive investments. Historically, lower interest rates have tended to favor growth investments over value. Meanwhile, passive fund flows tend to favor larger-cap companies, even those that nominally fall within our small-cap benchmark. We note, as of June 30, 2025, companies with a market cap of more than $5 billion accounted for 29% of the benchmark, versus just 14% on June 30, 2024. For the benchmark, we estimate that the $5 billion-plus market-cap cohort generated a 26.5% return during the quarter, versus an 11.7% return for companies with market caps below $2 billion.

From a sector perspective, stock selection in Information Technology and Industrials was the biggest contributor to performance. This was outweighed by negative stock selection in Materials, Financials, and Health Care, which were the biggest detractors during the quarter.

From a geographic perspective, stock selection in Italy and Belgium and allocation effects in Ireland were the biggest contributors to performance, however, stock selection in Switzerland and Australia and a lack of allocation to Israel were the biggest detractors.

Contributors and Detractors

There were several detractors to performance during the quarter. Ontex, a Belgium private label maker of diapers, adult incontinence, and feminine-care products, experienced weaker profitability due to unexpected cost increases and restructuring expenses. Nabtesco, a Japanese factory automation and hydraulic equipment maker, continued to experience weakness in its end-markets, especially China. Nakanishi, a leading global manufacturer of dental drills, experienced slowing orders due to tariff concerns. Husqvarna, a Swedish maker of robotic mowers and gardening equipment, is facing accelerating competition for new end-markets. Victrex, the largest manufacturer of high-end PEEK polymer, experienced weaker earnings due to tariff concerns and a weaker U.S. dollar.

There were also several top contributors. Spectris, a U.K.-listed maker of scientific test and measurement equipment, received a buyout offer from private equity. X-FAB Silicon Foundries, an analog semiconductor foundry, reported earnings and an outlook that exceeded expectations after several disappointing quarters. Tinexta, an IT services and digital-identity services provider for businesses, mostly in Italy, reported better-than-expected earnings. Anritsu, a Japanese maker of scientific test and measurement equipment is seeing end-markets rebound after a sustained downturn, and Serco Group, an outsourced facilities management and training services provider, continues to win military contracts in the U.S. and U.K.

Buys and Sells

During the quarter, we initiated eight new positions, as heightened volatility increased our overall opportunity set. One new position, AEON Financial Service Company (“AFS”), is the financial services arm of the Japanese retail giant, AEON. While most of AFS is geared toward Japanese consumers, the company’s Thai, Malaysian, Vietnamese, and Hong Kong subsidiaries capture consumer growth opportunities in some of the fastest-growing Asian economies. AFS benefits from cheap funding through AEON Bank deposits, which are recycled into consumer revolving loans, cash advance loans, and mortgages, while AFS continues to take market share in credit cards. Credit cards only have 43% penetration in Japan versus 80% in the U.S. Trading at about 60% of book value, we believe there are multiple potential avenues of value creation for this stock.

We also initiated a position in Spectris, a British test and measurement equipment provider. A mix of macro factors caused shares to reflect multiyear-low valuations, in our view. But with business activity showing signs of improvement, we believe the company could see a return to growth in the coming quarters. (After our entry, KKR and Advent both indicated a desire to acquire the business at a significant premium, which will likely result in a short but very favorable holding period for the portfolio.)

During the quarter we exited five positions, including ESR Group. ESR operates a real estate fund management business, primarily on e-commerce logistics and data centers. After receiving a buyout offer from a private equity consortium, we exited our position rather than risk a deal failure — a very real possibility in the current geopolitical environment. We also exited our position in Swedish gardening equipment firm Husqvarna, which we believe is likely to continue to struggle with its two-front war battling the increasing intensity of Chinese competitors and U.S. tariffs.

Sector Underweights and Overweights

During the quarter, Consumer Staples replaced Industrials as the second-largest overweight, and Consumer Discretionary replaced Financials as the second-largest underweight. As always, the portfolio’s sector allocations are a function of bottom-up stock selection consistent with our value-oriented philosophy and reflect specific idiosyncratic opportunities available to us rather than any active sector bet.

Outlook

Despite a sharp second quarter rebound, forward earnings multiples for the MSCI EAFE Small Cap Index, our proxy for non-U.S. developed market small caps, ended the quarter at a forward price-to-earnings (“P/E”) multiple of 15x, below the 10-year average of 18x. Other metrics such as price-to-book (“P/B”) and enterprise value divided by earnings before interest, taxes, depreciation, and amortization (“EV/EBITDA”) also indicate valuation levels that are at or below historical averages, while the second quarter dividend yield, at 3.3%, is above the 2.7% average. The MSCI EAFE Index indicates valuation levels that are elevated relative to the 10-year average on forward P/E, P/B, and EV/EBITDA, with dividend yields below average. Hence, relative to large-cap peers and their own history, non-U.S. developed market small caps still appear to be attractively priced, in our view.

The macroeconomic backdrop also continues to favor smaller foreign companies relative to larger ones. Relative to the U.S. dollar, most developed market currencies are strengthening despite central bank policies that are lowering monetary policy rates. We believe currencies were driven more by capital flows than interest rates in the second quarter and expect this dynamic to continue for the foreseeable future, as capital repatriation continues to drive asset flows out of the U.S. Stronger currencies tend to favor smaller-cap companies because they are less export-oriented. Meanwhile, lower interest rates, as most developed market central banks (except the Fed) loosen monetary policy, tend to disproportionately favor smaller companies because they tend to have higher levels of leverage.

At a granular level, we believe geopolitical tensions and tariffs favor smaller, more localized companies with local supply chains. Even the export-oriented companies in the portfolio that were impacted by previous trade tensions in 2017 have reconfigured their supply chains to limit tariff impacts.

Performance data shown represents past performance and is no guarantee of future results. Fund performance is net of management fees and expenses and reflects reinvested dividends and distributions. Past performance reflects the beneficial effect of any expense waivers or reimbursements, without which returns would have been lower. Investment returns and principal value will fluctuate and when redeemed may be worth more or less than their original cost. Returns for periods less than one year are not annualized. Current performance may be higher or lower and is available through the most recent month end at harborcapital.com or by calling 800-422-1050.

Important Information

Risks

There is no guarantee that the investment objective of the Fund will be achieved. Stock markets are volatile and equity values can decline significantly in response to adverse issuer, political, regulatory, market and economic conditions. Investing in international and emerging markets poses special risks, including potentially greater price volatility due to social, political and economic factors, as well as currency exchange rate fluctuations. These risks are more severe for securities of issuers in emerging market regions. Stocks of small cap companies pose special risks, including possible illiquidity and greater price volatility than stocks of larger, more established companies.

Benchmarks

The MSCI EAFE Small Cap (ND) Index is an equity index which captures small cap representation across developed market countries around the world, excluding the U.S. and Canada. This unmanaged index does not reflect fees and expenses and is not available for direct investment. Benchmark returns are adjusted for withholding taxes.

Disclosures

All holdings-related data is provided by FactSet. Because FactSet relies on external sources for its data, that data may differ slightly from actual values maintained by Harbor Funds.

Due to the security valuation procedures of the Fund and intra-day trading activity not included in the FactSet calculations, the actual returns may vary. From time to time the cash return in the portfolio may appear distorted based on the way FactSet’s attribution calculation methodology addresses delayed settlements.

Beta is a rolling three year, unless the Fund has a track record of less than three years, in which case it is a rolling one year. Best and Worst Performers sections reflect stocks in the portfolio for the quarter with an average weight of 0.25% or greater.

Views expressed herein are drawn from commentary provided to Harbor by the subadvisor and may not be reflective of their current opinions or future actions, are subject to change without prior notice, and should not be considered investment advice.

This information should not be considered as a recommendation to purchase or sell a particular security. The weightings, holdings, industries, sectors, countries, and returns mentioned may change at any time and may not represent current or future investments.

As a result of changing market conditions, total net asset levels, expenses and other statistics may change at any time and may differ from those shown.

The total amount shown for sector, industries, or country holdings may be greater than 100% because of the inclusion of derivatives and the collateral securities supporting those instruments. Sector allocations are determined using the Global Industry Classification Standard (GICS), which is a service of Morgan Stanley Capital International (MSCI) and Standard & Poor's (S&P).

Investors should carefully consider the investment objectives, risks, charges and expenses of a fund before investing. To obtain a summary prospectus or prospectus for this and other information, visit harborcapital.com or call 800-422-1050. Read it carefully before investing.

Distributed by Harbor Funds Distributors, Inc.

Attribution Disclosures

Linked Performance by Sectors data is produced from FactSet using data supplied by State Street Bank and Trust Company. Active Currency Contribution is the Currency Contribution of the portfolio minus the Currency Contribution of the benchmark.

Allocation Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return that is attributable to taking different group bets from the benchmark. (If either the portfolio or the benchmark has no position in a given group, allocation effect is the lone effect.) A group's allocation effect equals the average percent capitalization of the portfolio's group minus the average percent cap of the benchmark's group times the total return of the benchmark group minus the total return of the benchmark.

Average Weight is the dollar value (price times the shares held) of the security or group, divided by the total dollar value of the entire portfolio displayed as a percentage. It is calculated as the simple arithmetic average of daily values.

Contribution to Return is the contribution of a security or group to the overall portfolio return. It is calculated as the security weight multiplied by the daily security return linked daily across the reporting period. Currency Contribution is Total Return in USD subtracting out the Local Returns.

Local Returns are the Total Return of the portfolio or benchmark using the local currency.

Selection Effect is the portion of portfolio excess return attributable to choosing different securities within groups from the benchmark. A group's security selection effect equals the average weight of the benchmark's group times the total return of the portfolio's group minus the total return of the benchmark's group.

Total Effect is the sum of Allocation Effect and Selection Effect. The total effect represents the opportunity cost of what was done in a group relative to the overall portfolio. It is not just the difference between percent contribution in the portfolio and benchmark. At the overall portfolio level, the two numbers are equal. At the group level, they can be different.

Total Return is the price change of a security or group including dividends accrued over the report period (or the in-portfolio return) which includes only the time period that each security was in the portfolio.

Definitions

Beta is a measure of systematic risk, or the sensitivity of a fund to movements in the benchmark. A beta of 1 implies that the expected movement of a fund's return would match that of the benchmark used to measure beta.

Median Market Cap: The median size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares.

Weighted Average Market Capitalization: The average size of the companies in a portfolio or index as measured by the market value of outstanding shares. Earnings per share (EPS) is the portion of a company's profit allocated to each outstanding share.

The Est 3-5 Yr EPS Growth (%) is the estimated growth of earnings per share over the next 3-5 years, using pre-calculated mean long-term EPS growth rate estimates, which are calculated using each individual broker’s methodology, from FactSet, First Call, I/B/E/S Consensus, and Reuters. Forward looking estimates may not come to pass.

% EPS Growth - Past 3 Year: Earnings per share refers to the bottom-line measure of a company’s profitability defined as net income divided by the number of outstanding shares. The Adjusted Trailing P/E (price-to-earnings) Ratio is the closing stock price divided by the sum of the last 12 months actual EPS.

Forecasted P/E Ratio: a measure of the P/E (price-to-earnings) ratio using forecasted earnings for the P/E calculation. Return on Equity (ROE) is a measure of financial performance calculated by dividing net income by shareholders' equity. The Price/Book (price-to-book) Ratio evaluates a firm's market value relative to its book value.

All P/E, ROE and P/B statistics are calculated as weighted medians.
